words: Well, the first one here is Sleigh Hall Brown. Well, he was on a job that they one winter they called it the Sleigh Hall. He was getting logs out. So that name hung on to him. Sleigh Hall Brown. Then they had another guy Laughin' Mike. He laughed about all the time it seemed. Nobody knew him by his real name. Laughin' Mike. And then they had another fella with three fingernails. He was, I don't know, I think he was Sawyer at one time. Three fingers got off in one hand. He was three fingernails. Then they had another guy around there Cryin' Gus. He was mostly a cedar maker. You get to drinkin' a little bit and you cry. Laughing. They had another guy He was a fighter guy around there. He worked mostly with the horses. He was a teamster too. Samuel T. Red Rosie Joe.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Samuel T.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Red Rosie Joe?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Samuel T. Red Rosie Joe.

words: Where'd he ever get it?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, I don't know. People kept hangin' it on to him.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Then they had another guy, they called him the Chipmunk. He was Woodsworth of course. He was a very small gyppo yard. He'd gettin' out logs for different ones. He didn't do too well about it either. But he was the Chipmunk.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: When you mention any of those names, everybody there would know just who you were talkin' about.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That old sleigh -hull, as he got older He was kinda bullcookin' around the restaurant there when Mrs. Watts was running. Oh, they used to argue back and forth. She wanted him to go out and get in some wood. He said he was hungry and he wanted to eat first. She wanted him to get the wood in first. And his argument started. I think she threw a cup or somethin' at him. And they said he brushed up his sleighs and he says, I'll bombard your cabin. With a few cuss words in there. But then they got together about it. It was alright. I don't know who won out.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Did Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Watts have the boarding house for a long time?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It was a restaurant. They did have some rooms up overhead. But, oh yeah, many years. The restaurant. She was an old timer just like the rest of them there. She stayed with it a long time. Fed everybody. Never turned anybody away. Whether they had the money or not. So, she never got rich at it. But she lived. Well, that's all she was interested in. Nice woman. Very nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the other places that Lumberjack stayed in there in town?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, the hotel that was along on Main Street, they called it Spokane Hotel. That was the name of it. That was one place. Then the Bovill Hotel, part of that building was a log.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And, oh, there was earlier days Mrs. David used to have a boarding house which was both board and room. Not if you stayed there, but however, you see, that would just be one maybe in town. Because whenever they were working, they were out in the woods somewhere to camp.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Watches, they had some rooms over the restaurant. Well, they had a pool room there too. Billy Watts, husband of Mr. Watts from Ed, a pool room. And those rooms and the restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the pool room very popular?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yeah, it was. It was a popular place. Yeah. And there was one other pool room there also, Jimmy Gilroy. That was quite popular, but I believe Watches was the most popular.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The jacks seemed to hang around there. There was always card games going on. Whatnot.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And Watts in the earlier days, he used to work in the woods too. I think he was a donkey man.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They said they had a high line there one time. That's before my time. And that was with a donkey, a power. This high line, they'd transport the logs across that high line to where they wanted them. And they had a target there. Get over there to trip that. I believe for a while they used to shoot this target with a shotgun. Then it'd trip and that log would drop down. They wanted it. But then there was another time when something jammed. The log was up there, mid -air. No way of getting it down. And they said that this Billy Watts with a rifle, I don't know what the distance would be, but off by the waves. He shot repeatedly until he cut that chain in a big heavy chain. And he cut it in two. And the thing came loose and they were in business.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: But you'd have to shoot almost right exactly where you did before.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And I don't know, they must have had steel bullets too. They were shells because the old lead wouldn't do much with a steel chain. But it took a lot of shooting and he cut it in two.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: He was a good shot all the way with a gun. He was the only one playing pool. He was a good shot. It would have to be to accomplish a feat like that. Because that thing wouldn't be just motionless up there either, you know. Maybe a little swing was the way to it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I was going to ask you if on the whole, the Lumberjacks and townspeople hadn't had a lot to do with each other if they really were quite separate.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, they always got along, of course. There'd be a lot of Lumberjacks that didn't get very well acquainted with the townspeople. However, I suppose every Jack that ever came in and stayed over a weekend or so, they would get acquainted with at least one or two families. They were respected and all that. There was no reason why.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The townspeople knew them pretty well.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I was wondering what the town felt like when you were first there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you think back on it, what strikes your memory of the sights and the sounds in that town?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, of course it was a small town. The streets, I went there in April 1925. The streets, the main street was just sea of mud. It was just dirt streets is all they were, no gravel or rock of any kind. They were so muddy that the dray wagons were very near down to the axles pulling through there. It would remain that way until it dried more. It was that way a long time. That was the first and that was the same way for a couple of years or more. I think it was about 1929 before we got gravel on those streets. It made quite a difference. They couldn't do much about that either about those muddy streets but they used to have big drags there that floats, whatever they called them, packers of some kind, about four horses on them. They'd go over and over and over and it would help to pack down. But the top would dry and then if you had a load that was very heavy at all it would push on down. That didn't bother people. They knew that they had that every year I guess. We did that. Of course it was kind of a busy place for me.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Two railroads there and everything that came into the town or went out had to go by rail. So, they handled just about everything that there was.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, I don't know the size of that. At that time there was really only one industry right in town and that was Cedar Yard. I don't know how many people on average worked there but probably twenty or such. Twenty, twenty -five maybe.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That was the only thing right in town.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They had a high school there but they didn't have a gym. However, the gym was built shortly after we went there. I think it was started in twenty -five. Maybe men completed that same year. It was a brick gym. Quite an addition to the town. The kids before that more or less played basketball outdoors than the weather permit. So consequently they didn't have any kind of a team when they'd go to another town where they had a gym while the boho kids had no chance at all to compete.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It wasn't too long after they got that gym that they were right up there in competition with Penny and all of them. They had some pretty good basketball teams. Every once in a while a tournament would be held there. Most times you'd be packed every corner and you couldn't get another person in them.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It would be hard to even get them out if something happened in there like a fire. I don't know what had to happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an idea of what the train schedules was like? How much moved in and out of that town?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, on the WI&M we had what we called a logger that came to bowl early every morning and took the logs back to Potlatch that would have been cut and hauled into Bovill the day before. And then on the WI&M we had a passenger train which was a mixed train. They hauled some freight and passengers that made a round trip from Palouse up to Bovill back to Palouse and back to Potlatch. That was daily except Sunday. They were all daily except Sunday. And the Milwaukee, they didn't have a logger because there were no logs moving in that direction. They had a freight that would run to Elk River one day from St. Mary's and then back to St. Mary's the next day. But the Milwaukee also had a passenger train. That was strictly a passenger train. About three coaches, a mail car and an express car.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: That would go from St. Mary's to Elk River and return daily except Sunday.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Quite a few passengers. Anyway, it kept it going for several years. But as the depression was coming along probably about in 30 or something like that the passenger train was discontinued to Elk River.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Elk River had a good size sawmill there in Elk River. Right up to about 1930. Not exact here, but just about up to 1930. And it closed down.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And, I don't know when a year or more, maybe a couple of years afterwards, they burned it. Removed everything in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these railroad men pretty much a returnee of their own? Did they stick together in their own group?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: The ones that, well, the railroad, we didn't have anybody tie up in Bovill. The W &M crews all tied up in Potlatch and the Milwaukee would be tying up in St. Mary's. Or Elk River. It was just once in a while that we'd have a turnaround freight that would run between Bovill and Elk River that we'd tie up in Bovill. Of course, there was a lot of trainmen there that was trainmen for the Potlatch Lumber Company.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: They did the same work as the trainmen would do on any ordinary railroad, however, they were not

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: a carrier.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Lumber Company railroad workers, about all you could put them as, that thing, of course. Conductors, engineers, brakemen, so on. There were several crews of those and they were four or less family men that lived in Bovill.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Most all of them were family men. No, they were just ordinary citizens. They didn't congregate together in their leisure time. I guess they'd seen enough of each other while they were working.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I was thinking of that really because of the railroad lingo you know, the ways of talking about the railroads that I heard that they had.

speaker: Joseph Holland

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well, yes, especially at that time it was good. I guess it's still good there's fewer people working because of the longer trains and the diesel engines where they put so many units together. I don't know how many units they could put on one train with several, one engineer.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Trains anywhere from a mile to a mile and a half long.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: And the cars are all getting bigger. Where a 40 -foot car used to boxcar used to be considered a good sized car now it's a small one. Because they did have 36 -foot box before that now 50 and up.

words: What kind of size did they have then?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: because they might have to get the signal to see so many curves and what not no radio, which they have radio now, the conductor can set in his caboose and talk to the engineer with radio. Well before it was hand signals of course it was a train of boxcars or flat cars or something like that, the brakeman could go over the top and get to give signals to the head end. But where you have logs in between or poles or any commodity where a brakeman can't get over them, well that would be out then. Well they'd just have to get out and walk until they could get around the curve enough to where the head end could see them. So you could always get stopped and pull the air, everything stops. But they don't like to do that unless they have to because, oh they can get flat wheels from it, sudden stops.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh that's it, right there in Bovill now Bovill area, they have at least three big units, I guess sometimes more these units, logs tie as many behind them as they can pull

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Well they're the trainmen, they're the laws on them are changing some, it used to be 16 hours they called it the 16 hour law

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: employee couldn't work more than 16 hours Now it's dropped down to 14 a few years ago, I'm not so sure but maybe it's only 12 now, it's going to gradually get down I think the trainmen bucked that pretty strong too, but nevertheless it came How did the 16 hours work, was it 8 hours straight and then time and a half?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Yes, just about the way it was but they don't go by time, they go by the miles 100 miles is supposed to have been a day's work and that was figured out, if you figured it down it would have been about 8 and a half hours so the trainmen's time, the engineer too it's all so many miles, they don't put the hours down

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: It means the same thing, but still for all it was all it went by miles 100 miles roughly was supposed to be a day's work and then after that it was an inch and a half they'd call it so that's what they like to get in on on overtime Of course they had other stipulations in there too about switching any switching they had to do before they left a terminal was called terminal delay and that was more miles per hour than the ordinary running but switching in between terminals that was nothing more than just time consuming

words: you see a railroad agent is also express agent and that's the way the express company pays you on commission. You get 10 % of everything you handle Money orders, sales as well as express traffic so that's what I was getting. It would average about $210 a month which was good

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: because oh I don't know $125 a month was pretty fair wages for other types of work

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: that's about it

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: No, I was saying let's not have the names, but just the story

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh well, this conductor and engineer they just couldn't get along and still they had to work together I don't know what went out of it I don't know how long that went on but it would have been for months and this conductor told me, he said, I don't know what I figured I could do and it needs to kill him, he said, that's what I was going to do and this particular day when they tied up he went somewhere, I don't know, he went home, he went somewhere and he got a gun and he had to walk back to some part of the town, the edge of the town to get to where he was going to kill this engineer and he met a brakeman that he knew quite well and they talked and well this brakeman just talked him out of it it took a long time but he said when he got through talking, he said, I went back home and then it was out of my system and I didn't want to kill him after that

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: but I think they never were on good terms afterwards either but he wasn't quite as bad as before I think he, I think this brakeman sort of explained to him that well relations wasn't good but maybe he could still live with it if he tried so I think that's that was what he did afterwards just try to live with it I don't know why the man told me that to me, he was a fellow that you could believe too what he'd say

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: but he said I'd give him a lot of thought and I just thought that's the only thing I could do that's all there's left to do just kill him when I asked him, I said, did you ever think of what would happen to you?

words: No, he says that was never thought about that part

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: but when he didn't say it, it seemed that he was sure thanking this brakeman and just by accident that he ran out of this brakeman, just happened to meet up and it's the way to the engineers

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: the engineer and the fireman gets a little more time than the conductor or the brakeman when they do tie up they get a little time of inspection or something about the engine afterwards, and that's why the engineer would have been there a little longer

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: yep

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: that's the way that went Is it true that the conductor was over the engineer?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: whatever orders he'd give of course they would have to be within the rules if he tried to tell an engineer to go somewhere and the engineer knew the track was washed out or something, he wouldn't do it

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: but the conductor is in charge of the train and responsible for everything that happens

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: of course there could be a derailment or an accident, a crossing accident of some kind maybe an engineer might have been negligent about it well that wouldn't involve the conductor, he wouldn't be responsible for that because he'd been in no position to do anything about it but generally speaking, the conductor is in charge of the train

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: yep

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: yep yep yep

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: ah, little comical things happen sometimes they happen right in front of the depot there in Bovill the Milwaukee Railroad is a big railroad Chicago, Seattle and this WI&M is approximately 50 miles, well these two trains just switching and working around the whole thing, they came face to face on the main track and they do have some rules that who's supposed to have preference and all this and that but they just wasn't budging there for a while and nobody was going to give up who was going to back up and get out of there and this one WI&M brakeman, he sat down on the rail in front of the engine and told the others, brakeman, he said, well he says my railroad isn't as long as yours but it's just as wide

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: well well, before long the conductors got the other one, well they had no trouble with just these two brakemen they got a little stubborn

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were operating procedures uniform from railroad to railroad?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh yes yeah

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: they were, very very little difference

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: the rule book was the rule book and it seemed to fly wherever you went

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've been told that one guy said that he wished that he got into railroad work instead of logging because he felt he would have had more security and better pay keeping that straight?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: well, it would depend, you know if he could have worked steadily, well yes he'd have had more pay because the woods there are a few good high paying jobs in the woods but they, apparently speaking, they lose quite a little bit of time every year for weather and what not so railroad, if you could work steady but then you'd be up against that seniority again, you know new men starting out would have to expect to not be able to work steady

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: oh yeah, I would I don't think it would be much better if somebody started on the railroad and would be starting with the woods because with railroad work you can generally work right on as you get older you're probably a little more valuable to the company through your experience and there isn't that kind of physical strength that you have to have that would as compared to the woods the woods, you you have to be pretty able bodied you know, to hit that hard work all the time stand up under it, you get a little old, you've got to get out of there and there's no place to go, except for the few you know, that will get to be supervised of some kind, but that's a small percentage

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: so the woods is really no place for an old man, but on the railroads train service, I guess they're getting a little better about it now, but it was really horrible because they had no rules governing it and some of those old engineers, you know, pushing 80 years old still running those engines, and they couldn't get them off of there but these last years though, they are doing something along with that

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: it was hard for the unions to do anything, sure the young fellas wanted to get the old ones off but there wasn't enough young fellas for the votes to the union, more old guys than there were young they kept those the engineers, they hung onto theirs longer than conductors finally, then they compromised about, oh the forced retirement, we'll say, was 70 years alright, next year it'll be 69, and the following year 68, and then 67, then 66, then 65 go down to 65, but it'd take about 5 years to do that so, something along that line they went for that, because it just didn't throw everybody out that was over 65 you see, it gave them a little time to think about it so, I don't know but I believe maybe right now it's gotten down to where forced retirement on the railroad probably is 65 or not much more years ago they didn't have any and they should have

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: my wife had an alcoholic who was an engineer on the Milwaukee who lived in Mobridge, South Dakota a long time, and he got to be number one engineer and seniority on the railroad so his last years were strictly on passenger trains and he visited us one winter, he stayed all winter there after he'd retired and his wife passed away and we were talking there I said, oh, Uncle Jim that's what the wife called us, you probably went to sleep too run those engines, never he says, never, and he turned around he says, on passengers, on passengers yeah, he says, I have on freight and then he started telling them about a freight train I don't know why, he says, everybody on that train went to sleep, and we went through a station and we were about 8 miles beyond it when somebody woke up when he woke up, he didn't know where he was at and then they got the others awake, but it was at night and they did get back in they backed into this station and they covered it up to where it never was reported and we said, did he can to all of us?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: he says, I don't know oh yeah engineers aren't on freights they used to say, I've seen one you start going up a grade, then they'll go to sleep but just as soon as that engine pitches over the top they wake right up, the difference in the working of the engine

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: and get on the next one, they do the same well, they work 16 hours and then they have to eat and you just can't jump into bed the minute after you eat you know, you're going to take a little time or something or other and then in the morning, you've only got 8 hours in between in the morning you'll have to get up in time shave, do whatever you have to do and eat breakfast before you go to work, so I don't know if they kept their sleeping time down to 5 and a half, 6 hours I suppose

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: so you can see why they get pretty sleepy well, it's alright as long as everybody doesn't go to sleep at the same time that's the bad one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how many days would they work before they'd get off?

speaker: Joseph Holland

words: but in the old days these guys would be working, they'd work 5 days and then have a weekend or would it be even worse than that?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: but some of the branch lines wouldn't be running on Sundays, but the main line main line passenger trains Sunday was just another day and they never got any extra pay for it either Saturday or Sunday, pay was the same as it was on Monday

words: of course the passenger trains carried the mail and express and that all helped

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I think that's exactly what it was

words: at fifty miles it would be two fifty but it seems that there was two sixty -five I think a fare like that would discourage people from using it unless they needed to that was the only way to get there no buses, the only other way would be to walk had very few cars in the country at the time and then for so many of the winter months from Old Lake in October to well out in May there was no cars getting out of town at all dirt roads wherever you go to and going through the woods whichever direction you tried to go in and they were wet and soggy and stuck with no gravel on them so people there are all about well I'd say late in October on an average year they'd put their cars up on blocks

words: a piece of cordwood they'd sit there

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: there was one storekeeper there, old John Grohl he was an old pioneer he told me about a family that lived at Camp 8, well Camp 8 was roughly a mile and a half north of Baltimore but they called it Camp 8 one time it had a schoolhouse there, there was a little settlement and the shops were there but there was two families that moved in there, kind of in September and they started trading with his store and two families that bought quite a lot of stuff and paid for it and then this John Grohl used to deliver out there I guess it was once a week, had a team packed that he delivered with, a young lad doing it but these two couples, the men I suppose it was the men, come in and talked after they'd run credit for a month and then they'd come in and paid for it and he thought he had a couple of real good customers there and they said, well now, winter's coming on and I know you probably won't like it me coming out there any more than you have to, so maybe if we could lay in the heavier commodities, there'd be light stuff yes, so John he thought that'd be a darn good idea so they loaded up heavy, everything they got pretty good and then about a week or so went by, they generally was down there at least once a week but about a week went by and none of them showed up so he inquired, oh no they'd left three or four days ago they went out the other way he never said any more

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: but now those people wouldn't have been lumberjacks they'd just come in there to do that kind of a job you know

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: winter supply of groceries I don't know he couldn't get any trace on them anyway of course, you know, the phone system wasn't much if any, they had so much solutions and then head start on you you wouldn't know you

words: I've got one more thing that I want to ask you, and that's the story of the clock here how you came to get it and how it came, what you know about it.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: Oh, well, of course the clock was when I was eight and above when the clock was installed we had another clock, and it went haywire and they sent it down to Potlatch and they had a jeweler to tell you that Potlatch, and he looked the other one over now and he said, it's just worn out, there's no need to try and do anything with that, so they got a new one that was this one I presume it was new, it looked new they sent it up to Bovill, and it was there all those years, 40 at least and then when I retired and told the agent, now that's a 30 day clock and it hadn't been stopped in many years it hung up kind of high, the ceilings there in the depot were real high, but I told him just how I did it an easy job if you knew how to wind it yeah, well I was gone three months and when I got back to Bovill one of the first things I done was to go down to the depot and there the clock was stopped, and I said George, you didn't wind that clock, no and he says that's only half of it, he says I'm not going to wind it he says how come, he says you're the only one that's ever wound that clock, well I really hadn't thought of that myself but when I got to thinking back, yes, I was the only one that ever wound that clock because on my vacations, I never did get any three weeks, three weeks was the most I ever got and I would always wait until after the first of the month to get the monthly reports out of the way before I would go on vacation and arrange to be back before the end of the month to get them out, so I'd wind the clock before I could go and then he said I'll run when I get back and no, and he said it's your clock well I said, how do you know it is well, he says the WI&M said it's your clock, and well I said no, the WI&M hasn't got all to say about this, the Milwaukee owns half of that clock, the WI&M can't just give it to me, well alright, he says I'll contact the Milwaukee, he said you come back in a few days, we'll see what they say so I come back in a few days or a week, okay, he says I've cleared with the Milwaukee he says it's your clock so he says you better take it before someone else comes along and takes it, so okay, I took the clock home and that's how I happen to have it well I sold the house at Bovill and the wife of the people that bought it and I'm looking the house over, and of course she says the clock will go with it and then she chuckles a little bit when she sees my face oh, she says I know I couldn't be that lucky so that's the clock had that clock been stopped before during the years you were at Bovill?

words: I never have known it to have stopped.

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I wasn't goofing off the way it was it was on a Saturday and I had the cashier and I had the baggage man and this fellow was a sheep man and he had I think about 8 carloads of sheep to load and get on this train that left at 145 well this train was also the mail train the passenger and they had a connection at Palouse that they wanted to make by all means, you know, because if you don't get the mail through well, you know, pretty soon, maybe you won't have the contract so that's important, but this day, now sheep I'm no sheep man, but I've watched some of them load some days, and nobody knows why, but some days those sheep, they'll have one sheep go up, especially in the top deck see there's two decks, the lower deck they go up pretty good in them, but the upper deck is a little higher, and they'll take a bell sheep up there, you know and then those others, if they get up there part way they'll stand and feed back down, and you almost have to carry them in until you get a certain number in, and then they'll come but then there's other days that they'll walk right up there, and nobody knows why that is they do it the same way each time but there's those days they just load fine, and then there's other days they fight getting those cars, well all these guys were working out there, all of them, and the section crew was working too and they weren't supposed to, this was the sheep man they were supposed to load those sheep, but when we had the section crew around there, working around the yards that day, and I asked the foreman it's going to be tough, I said, getting those sheep loaded and get them out, because you'd load a car, and then you'd have to move it by hand well they have track movers and barge and whatnot, you know, you'd have to move it up and then get another empty in there, and they were loading so slow this day, so slow and it was on a Saturday, and this man had to get permission to bring all those sheep in, you know and he'd been an unlawful fix, because there wasn't a train until Monday, no feed there, he'd have to he'd have to take them back out, and he'd have to get permission from people and lock a lot of damages down, you know, gardens and whatnot so, oh, I was every second night, I was out there helping, the baggies was helping the cashier was helping, and we were all, you know, trying to get those sheep out, and well, this gambler he called me, how are they coming, well, I'd say pretty good now, I don't think it'll be long, well you can hold maybe 20 minutes more okay, I knew that 20 minutes was nothing, you know, they couldn't possibly be left for 20 minutes, and called me back, so I called him back no, there's still three or four cars yet to load we can't wait, now he says, you turn that train loose at, and he gave me a time, well it was 2 .20 or 2 .30, whatever it was, he says, you turn that train loose at 2 .30, we'll say, well I was about 27 years old then, I suppose, and the conductor was right there, side to me, and I took the clearance that the conductor has to have, or he can leave on, and I just folded that up and put it in my best pocket, started out for the stockyards which was just out the other end of the depot the conductor just grinning, because he was nothing on him, he couldn't go until he had that clearance and oh, I tell you, we worked like slaves out there and I never went back into that depot, this was a report that didn't happen and we got the last sheep loaded got him out of there, when I got back in, of course the phone was ringing, I knew it would be, you know and I couldn't get ya, no I said, it wasn't in here, when did the train get out? I says, just now, I can still see him going, he said, didn't I tell you to turn that train loose at 2 .30?

speaker: Joseph Holland
words: I said, you sure did, Mr. Gamble Zingo, down the receiver went and I really thought, well I guess come Monday I'll be out of a job but you know, when you're that young, you don't worry too much about it I knew I could do the work somewhere else if I couldn't work there so, alright, Monday, passenger train comes in, Mr. Gamble is the first one to step off of the passenger came in, my baggage man and cashier was out there working the train, which they always did I stayed inside and he came in, and we talked about everything under the sun, politics baseball or what not, but he never once mentioned a sheep, not a word was said about that train Saturday of course I was really thankful, and I wasn't about to ring it up but he never said one word well, I stayed late that night to be sure, and I made a few phone calls through to Palouse, and I had the assurance that they would hold for that connection, you see, I knew that and I had worked at Palouse before, over that railroad, and I thought I could depend on them and sure enough, that train went through the sheep moved, the connection was made, the passengers made the passenger there, and them ale and everything and besides, they made little money, you know you carload the sheep, and satisfying a customer, you might get his next sheep or the next year, well I guess over the weekend, Mr. Gamble probably thought about that a little bit well, everything worked out alright so he never said a word, not one word and I was really, he'd have been justified, he actually would have been justified, no if you can't take orders you can't work here, he'd have been justified in doing it, but I was so set in getting those sheep out that nothing was going to stop him, and it didn't on a Monday morning train, I thought it was however, he used to come up though about once a week or sometimes a couple of weeks he'd be away sometimes, oh and he didn't come up that often no, I thought it was a little rare, but on the other hand I sort of expected him, I really expected him but I am telling you so astonished because he was so nice about everything we just talked about a little business, but then we talked about a couple of things too, we talked about everything but whatever happened Saturday, not a word I wonder if he had decided in his mind for sure before he got up there and talked to you oh, I'm sure he did I'm sure that, you see, he had the rest of that day Saturday and all day Sunday and half a day Monday oh I'm sure, when he seen that that train got through and made the connection and another thing for the train being a little late didn't cost him a penny of overtime for the train crew, because that crew and I don't know if they still have it or not, it had an unusual deal, they paid him ten hours for a day's work, if they worked over five, yeah that was it worked over five hours, you get paid for ten well they always tried to get their work done seven or eight hours never worked the ten, but they paid them for ten but if you worked the full ten, you got no more than if you worked five and a half hours, so it never cost them a penny extra for a train crew engine crew and the connection made, the sheep got on their way, the mail went through the passengers went through, everything so he had all that to think about, you know and then a little money that came from it you took a big gamble, I did, but you know, again I don't know, when you're young, it's different I wouldn't have done that same thing twenty -five years afterwards, no but I don't suppose I would but at that time, I did but you see, I had a good reason to think that everything would work out alright, as far as making that connection down there because those people told me we'll hold of course anything could happen on the way down if there had been a derailment or a delay, you know an hour or more, no, I couldn't have held because that train, the fluce, had to reach Spokane by a certain time to make the connections, you see, passenger connections were released, but everything worked no, he was really justified, I think if he had told me well, that's it, sorry, I said okay
